rulings.Case Title: ETHAN CHAN VS. HOWARD J. LEWIS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer To Pltf'S Second Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Real Property/Housing Court Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 8, 2017 line 4. Demurrer To Pltf'S Second Amended Complaint Sustained in part and overruled in part. The demurrer is overruled as to the first cause of action alleged against Mr. Lewis as trustee of the Jennie V. Maybon Trust. The demurrer is sustained without leave to amend as to the sixth, seventh, and eighth causes of action alleged against Mr. Lewis both individually and in his capacity as trustee. Apart from the court's October 18, 2016 order on Mr. Lewis' demurrer to the first amended complaint and the propriety of the Chans' amendment adding three additional causes of action, the Chans' opposition to this motion is treated as a request for leave to amend. As to the first cause of action, the Chans adequately allege a claim for negligent misrepresentation against Mr. Lewis in his capacity as trustee of the Maybon Trust. The complaint indicates Defendant RE/MAX was acting as an agent of Mr. Lewis as trustee when advertising the property. Any alleged misrepresentations are attributable to Mr. Lewis as principal of its agent RE/MAX. As to the first cause of action alleged against Mr. Lewis individually, there are no facts alleging Mr. Lewis made misrepresentations in his individual capacity and it is not reasonably possible this defect can be cured by amendment. The sixth cause of action does not allege a cognizable negligent nondisclosure claim because Mr. Lewis was under no duty to disclose facts within the reach of the diligent attention and observation of the buyer. The court in Newhall Land and Farming Co. v. Superior Court (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 334, 349, stated "where the seller knows of facts materially affecting the value or desirability of the property¿and also knows that such facts are not known to, or within the reach of the diligent attention and observation of the buyer, the seller is under a duty to disclose them to the buyer." Assuming without deciding Labor Code 2750.5 is inapplicable here, the seventh cause of action fails to allege a cognizable negligence claim because property sold "as-is" relieves the seller of all liability for defective conditions other than liability based on fraud, misrepresentation, or nondisclosure. (Shapiro v. Hu (1986) 188 Cal.App.3d 324, 332.) The eighth cause of action fails to allege a cognizable negligence per se claim and the Chans do not oppose the demurrer as to this cause of action.= (501/REQ)
